critique.summary: The article announces the release of FFmpeg 8.1 "Hoare" and summarizes recent major releases and updates to the FFmpeg multimedia framework. It highlights new codec support, Vulkan compute-based codecs, hardware acceleration features, and internal improvements across several versions.
mainPoints: FFmpeg 8.1 "Hoare" introduces new decoders including experimental xHE-AAC Mps212 and MPEG-H decoding, EXIF metadata parsing, and Vulkan compute-based codecs for ProRes and DPX.

FFmpeg 8.0 "Huffman" added native decoders for APV, ProRes RAW, RealVideo 6.0, and Vulkan compute-based codecs for FFv1 and ProRes RAW decoding, with hardware accelerated encoding and decoding for various codecs.
FFmpeg 7.1 "Péter" stabilized the VVC decoder, added native AAC USAC decoder support, MV-HEVC decoding, and Vulkan encoding for H264 and HEVC.
The project has modernized infrastructure, improved multi-threading in the ffmpeg CLI, and enhanced codec and filter support over recent releases.
FFmpeg continues to recommend users upgrade to the latest stable releases unless using the current git master branch.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reflects the official FFmpeg release notes and announcements as found on the FFmpeg website and corroborated by other reputable sources. The technical details about codec support, Vulkan compute-based codecs, hardware acceleration, and infrastructure modernization align with known FFmpeg development history and public changelogs. The dates and version numbers correspond to the official FFmpeg release timeline. There is no indication of misinformation or unsupported claims.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential compatibility issues or migration challenges for users upgrading from older FFmpeg versions.
There is no mention of licensing considerations or patent issues that sometimes affect codec usage in FFmpeg.
Performance benchmarks or comparative analysis with other multimedia frameworks are not provided.
The article assumes technical familiarity with video codecs, hardware acceleration, and Vulkan API, which may limit accessibility for general audiences.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the new FFmpeg 8.1 release, highlighting its extensive new features and improvements, user experiences, and some skepticism about hardware encoding support. There is also a desire expressed for simpler usage and scripting capabilities.

comments.author: gyan
comments.text:
Changelog:

ffprobe -codec option

EXIF Metadata Parsing

gfxcapture: Windows.Graphics.Capture based window/monitor capture

hxvs demuxer for HXVS/HXVT IP camera format

MPEG-H 3D Audio decoding via mpeghdec

D3D12 H.264 encoder

drawvg filter via libcairo

ffmpeg CLI tiled HEIF support

D3D12 AV1 encoder

ProRes Vulkan hwaccel

DPX Vulkan hwaccel

Rockchip H.264/HEVC hardware encoder

Add vf_scale_d3d12 filter

JPEG-XS parser

JPEG-XS decoder and encoder through libsvtjpegxs

JPEG-XS raw bitstream muxer and demuxer

IAMF Projection mode Ambisonic Audio Elements muxing and demuxing

Add vf_mestimate_d3d12 filter

xHE-AAC Mps212 decoding support (experimental)

Remove the old HLS protocol handler

Vulkan compute codec optimizations

swscale Vulkan support

LCEVC metadata bitstream filter

Add vf_deinterlace_d3d12 filter

ffprobe: only show refs field in stream section when reading frames

ProRes Vulkan encoder

LCEVC parser

LCEVC enhancement layer exporting in MPEG-TS
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate list of new features in FFmpeg 8.1, directly reflecting the official changelog.

comments.author: edgarvaldes
comments.text: One of the best open-source tools out there. I'm a frequent user of Plex, Jellyfin, Tunarr, local music files, etc. I use it weekly to extract subtitles, trim videos, convert music formats, and remove audio tracks. After writing the previous paragraph, I realized I've never donated to the project; it's time to change that.
comments.analysis: The comment shares a credible personal endorsement and practical use cases, adding valuable user perspective without exaggeration.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
FFmpeg is really great. The only wish I'd have is for the usage to become simpler - both for regular stuff, but also for advanced filtering.

If anyone remembers, avisynth was pretty cool back in the days. You could kind of script video/audio manipulations, a bit like a UNIX/Linux pipe, but kind of simpler, in my opinion. FFmpeg allows for many similar operations, but remembering anything here is ... hard. I'd love for the whole usage API to become much simpler, but it seems nobody among the ffmpeg dev team is considering this. :(

I can't be the only one with that wish though ...

It does not diminish ffmpeg being so great in general, but I think it could be better.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a well-reasoned critique about FFmpeg's complexity, reflecting common user sentiment and suggesting improvements.

comments.author: megous
comments.text: I wouldn't get too excited about rockchip hw encoding. It's rkmpp based, not an upstream solution. You'd need Rockchip kernel for this, I guess.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a cautious and technically informed view on hardware encoding support, highlighting potential limitations.

comments.author: pandaforce
comments.text: Khronos published a post on the Vulkan compute codecs in FFmpeg: https://www.khronos.org/blog/video-encoding-and-decoding-wit...
comments.analysis: This comment shares a relevant external resource that supports the discussion about Vulkan codec improvements.

comments.author: brcmthrowaway
comments.text: How much of this release was done by to corporate/big tech employees?
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid question about contributor demographics but lacks evidence or elaboration.
